title: Charge-Nurse ASSIGNMENT-MAKING, DELEGATION, and BEHAVIORAL-ESCALATION Leadership on a 20-Bed Adult Inpatient PSYCHIATRIC Unit: Matching Patient Acuity to Staff SCOPE (RN vs LPN/LVN vs UAP/Psych Tech), the FIVE RIGHTS of Delegation, What the RN CANNOT Delegate (Assessment, Evaluation, Initial Teaching, Unstable Patients), Leading VERBAL DE-ESCALATION FIRST with the Least-Restrictive Continuum (Oral PRN Before IM, Restraint/Seclusion LAST), and Staff/Environmental Safety During a Behavioral Emergency
subtitle: This is a LEADERSHIP, DELEGATION, and SAFE-ASSIGNMENT case (distinct from cases that teach the clinical care of a single psychiatric diagnosis). A day-shift CHARGE RN on a 20-bed adult inpatient psychiatric unit (census 18) leads a team of one additional staff RN, one LPN/LVN, and two psychiatric technicians (UAP) across a varied-acuity board — a newly admitted patient on 1:1 CONSTANT OBSERVATION for active suicidal ideation with a plan, a patient in ACUTE MANIA (bipolar I) who is escalating, a patient with first-episode psychosis responding to internal stimuli, a patient with borderline personality disorder testing limits, a patient in alcohol withdrawal on CIWA monitoring, and several stable patients pending discharge. The two intertwined competencies are SAFE DELEGATION/ASSIGNMENT and BEHAVIORAL DE-ESCALATION LEADERSHIP. DELEGATION follows the FIVE RIGHTS — the RIGHT TASK, under the RIGHT CIRCUMSTANCES, to the RIGHT PERSON, with the RIGHT DIRECTION/COMMUNICATION, and the RIGHT SUPERVISION/EVALUATION — and the RN ALWAYS retains ACCOUNTABILITY for the outcome even when a task is delegated. SCOPE OF PRACTICE governs who may do what: the RN performs and is solely responsible for ASSESSMENT, ANALYSIS, care PLANNING, EVALUATION of responses, INITIAL patient teaching, and the care of UNSTABLE or unpredictable patients and any task requiring clinical JUDGMENT — these are NEVER delegated; the LPN/LVN may administer most routine PO/IM/SQ medications, REINFORCE previously taught content, collect/REPORT data, and care for STABLE patients with predictable outcomes, under RN supervision; the UAP/psychiatric technician may perform CONSTANT 1:1 OBSERVATION (with clear parameters and RN oversight), obtain VITAL SIGNS, assist with ADLs/hygiene, transport, and help maintain a calm MILIEU, but never assessment, teaching, evaluation, or medication titration. SAFE ASSIGNMENT-MAKING matches ACUITY to COMPETENCY: the RN keeps the highest-acuity/least-predictable patients (the new 1:1 suicidal patient's risk assessment and safety plan, the escalating manic patient, the first-episode-psychosis assessment) and delegates stable, predictable work appropriately. BEHAVIORAL DE-ESCALATION uses the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE continuum: recognize the ESCALATION cycle (triggering → escalation → crisis) EARLY; lead VERBAL DE-ESCALATION FIRST — a single calm spokesperson, respect personal space, lower environmental stimulation, listen and validate, set clear limits, and offer realistic CHOICES including an ORAL PRN medication (oral is less restrictive than IM); ensure STAFF and ENVIRONMENTAL SAFETY (summon help/a behavioral response, keep your own egress and do NOT corner the patient, remove other patients and dangerous objects, never confront alone); escalate to IM emergency medication ONLY when oral is refused and danger is imminent (with monitoring of sedation/airway/EPS); and reserve SECLUSION/RESTRAINT as the absolute LAST resort, requiring a time-limited, non-PRN provider order, continuous monitoring, and a post-event debrief. The charge nurse uses CLOSED-LOOP communication, supervises and EVALUATES delegated work, reassesses assignments after the event, and leads a post-incident DEBRIEF and documentation. NURSING (CHARGE) ROLE: make safe, scope-appropriate assignments; delegate by the five rights while retaining accountability; protect the highest-acuity patients personally; lead least-restrictive behavioral de-escalation; safeguard staff and the milieu; and supervise, evaluate, debrief, and document.

patient.name: Charge RN — Adult Psychiatric Unit
patient.code: Charge-nurse leadership scenario; goal = safe scope-appropriate assignments, delegation by the five rights (RN retains accountability), protection of the highest-acuity patients, least-restrictive behavioral de-escalation, and staff/milieu safety
patient.admitDate: Day shift on a 20-bed adult inpatient psychiatric unit (census 18). The charge RN leads a team of one additional staff RN, one LPN/LVN, and two psychiatric technicians (UAP). Board: Bed 4 — a patient admitted overnight on 1:1 CONSTANT OBSERVATION for active suicidal ideation with a plan; Bed 7 — 'Mr. D,' 34, bipolar I in ACUTE MANIA, now ESCALATING (pacing the hall, loud and intrusive, demanding the phone, fists intermittently clenching, will not sit for breakfast); Bed 9 — first-episode psychosis, guarded, responding to internal stimuli; Bed 12 — borderline personality disorder, splitting staff and testing limits; Bed 15 — alcohol withdrawal on CIWA-Ar monitoring; plus several stable patients pending discharge. The charge nurse must make safe assignments, delegate appropriately, and lead the de-escalation of Mr. D before it reaches crisis.
patient.room: Locked inpatient psychiatric unit; interdisciplinary team (psychiatrist/NP, charge RN, staff RN, LPN/LVN, psychiatric technicians, social work); behavioral-response procedure and least-restrictive de-escalation protocol available; seclusion/restraint only by time-limited provider order as last resort

vitals.time: Mr. D — baseline acute mania (escalation beginning)
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; pacing the hallway, pressured loud speech, intrusive toward peers, demanding the phone, will not sit for breakfast — EARLY escalation phase: the moment to lead verbal de-escalation and lower stimulation, NOT to restrain

37.0°C; voice raised, fists clenched, invading a peer's space, refusing redirection — crisis is imminent: summon help/behavioral response, ensure staff egress and remove other patients/objects, single calm spokesperson, offer an ORAL PRN and clear choices

37.0°C; one staff member speaks calmly and respects personal space while others quietly clear the dayroom; he is listening, still keyed up but no longer advancing — least-restrictive verbal intervention is working; continue and offer the oral PRN

vitals.time: Mr. D — accepted ORAL PRN, regaining control
vitals.temp: 37.0°C; accepted oral olanzapine (least-restrictive medication route) and a quiet low-stimulation space; pacing slowing, speech less pressured — IM emergency medication and restraint were appropriately AVOIDED because oral + verbal de-escalation succeeded
37.0°C; calm, conversational, resting in his room; other patients returned to the dayroom; no injury to patient, peers, or staff — de-escalation resolved without seclusion or restraint

37.0°C; charge RN leads a post-incident debrief with staff and (when ready) the patient, documents the event and delegated tasks, and reassesses assignments and the 1:1 coverage for the remainder of the shift — closed-loop, supervised, accountable leadership

labs.n: SCOPE OF PRACTICE — RN vs LPN/LVN vs UAP/psychiatric technician
labs.v: Safe delegation begins with SCOPE OF PRACTICE — knowing what each team member may legally and competently do. The REGISTERED NURSE (RN) performs and is solely responsible for ASSESSMENT, ANALYSIS/clinical judgment, care PLANNING, EVALUATION of patient responses, INITIAL patient teaching, and the care of UNSTABLE, complex, or unpredictable patients (e.g., a newly admitted actively suicidal patient, an escalating manic patient, a first-episode-psychosis assessment) — these are NEVER delegated. The LICENSED PRACTICAL/VOCATIONAL NURSE (LPN/LVN) works under RN supervision and may administer most routine PO/IM/SQ medications, REINFORCE teaching the RN already initiated, perform ongoing data COLLECTION and REPORTING, and care for STABLE patients with PREDICTABLE outcomes (LPN/LVN scope generally excludes initial assessment, IV-push high-risk meds in many states, care planning, and patient teaching of new content). The UNLICENSED ASSISTIVE PERSONNEL (UAP)/PSYCHIATRIC TECHNICIAN may perform CONSTANT 1:1 OBSERVATION (with clear parameters and RN oversight), obtain VITAL SIGNS, assist with ADLs/hygiene/meals, transport, and help maintain a calm MILIEU — but NEVER assessment, evaluation, teaching, clinical judgment, or medication titration. Matching the right task to the right scope is the foundation of every safe assignment.
labs.r: RN ONLY: assessment, analysis/judgment, planning, EVALUATION, INITIAL teaching, UNSTABLE/complex patients — NEVER delegated. LPN/LVN (under RN): routine PO/IM/SQ meds, REINFORCE teaching, data collection/reporting, STABLE/PREDICTABLE patients. UAP/tech: 1:1 observation (with parameters + RN oversight), vitals, ADLs, transport, milieu — NO assessment/teaching/evaluation/judgment/titration. Charge role: match task to scope.

labs.n: THE FIVE RIGHTS OF DELEGATION (+ RN retains accountability)
labs.v: Delegation is the transfer of responsibility for performing a TASK to another team member while the RN RETAINS ACCOUNTABILITY for the OUTCOME. It is guided by the FIVE RIGHTS: (1) RIGHT TASK — a routine, predictable task within the delegatee's scope/competency; (2) RIGHT CIRCUMSTANCE — an appropriate, stable setting/patient; (3) RIGHT PERSON — the delegatee is competent and trained for THIS task with THIS patient; (4) RIGHT DIRECTION/COMMUNICATION — a clear, specific description of the task, expected outcome, limits, and what/when to report (closed-loop); and (5) RIGHT SUPERVISION/EVALUATION — the RN monitors performance, intervenes as needed, and EVALUATES the result. Crucially, delegating a task does NOT transfer accountability: if the RN delegates 1:1 observation to a technician, the RN remains accountable for the patient's safety and must give clear parameters and follow up. Tasks involving the nursing PROCESS (assessment, analysis, planning, evaluation), INITIAL teaching, clinical JUDGMENT, or UNSTABLE patients CANNOT be delegated regardless of how busy the unit is.
labs.r: FIVE RIGHTS: right TASK, right CIRCUMSTANCE, right PERSON, right DIRECTION/communication (clear + closed-loop), right SUPERVISION/EVALUATION. RN RETAINS ACCOUNTABILITY even after delegating. CANNOT delegate: assessment, analysis, planning, EVALUATION, INITIAL teaching, judgment, UNSTABLE patients. Being busy never justifies unsafe delegation. Charge role: delegate by the five rights + supervise/evaluate.

labs.n: SAFE ASSIGNMENT-MAKING — match ACUITY to COMPETENCY
labs.v: Making assignments is delegation at the unit level. The charge nurse matches each patient's ACUITY, predictability, and care needs to the team member's SCOPE and demonstrated COMPETENCY. The RN keeps for licensed-RN care the HIGHEST-acuity and LEAST-predictable patients: the newly admitted actively suicidal patient (initial risk assessment + safety plan), the escalating manic patient (active de-escalation + medication decisions), and the first-episode-psychosis patient (assessment of a changing/guarded presentation). STABLE, predictable patients (e.g., those pending discharge, the cooperative patient on maintenance medications) can be assigned to the LPN/LVN within scope, with the RN remaining available for assessment/teaching/evaluation. Continuous OBSERVATION, vitals, ADLs, and milieu support can be assigned to the psychiatric technicians with clear parameters. The charge nurse also balances workload, avoids assigning two highly demanding patients to one person, considers continuity, and stays free enough to respond to emergencies and supervise the team. Assignments are reassessed as acuity changes (e.g., after Mr. D's escalation).
labs.r: Assignment = unit-level delegation. Match ACUITY/predictability to SCOPE + COMPETENCY. RN keeps the high-acuity/unpredictable (new SI risk assessment, escalating mania, first-episode-psychosis assessment). LPN/LVN: stable/predictable within scope. UAP: observation/vitals/ADLs/milieu with parameters. Balance workload, keep charge available to supervise + respond, REASSESS as acuity changes. Charge role: safe acuity-to-competency matching.

labs.v: A high-yield safety principle: certain responsibilities CANNOT be delegated to an LPN/LVN or UAP because they require the RN's education and clinical JUDGMENT. These include the steps of the NURSING PROCESS that require judgment — ASSESSMENT (including the initial and any focused/changing assessment), ANALYSIS/nursing diagnosis, care PLANNING, and EVALUATION of the patient's response to interventions — as well as INITIAL patient/family TEACHING of new content, the care of UNSTABLE or unpredictable patients, and decisions requiring clinical judgment (e.g., titrating a CIWA-based benzodiazepine, deciding to administer an emergency medication, judging suicide risk). An LPN/LVN may REINFORCE teaching the RN already provided and collect/report data, but the RN interprets that data and makes the clinical decisions. A UAP may report observations (e.g., 'he's pacing and yelling') but does NOT assess, interpret, or decide. When a task is unstable, evaluative, judgment-laden, or involves initial teaching — the RN does it.
labs.r: RN CANNOT delegate: ASSESSMENT (initial/focused/changing), ANALYSIS/diagnosis, PLANNING, EVALUATION of response, INITIAL teaching, UNSTABLE-patient care, and JUDGMENT calls (med titration, emergency-med decision, suicide-risk determination). LPN/LVN may REINFORCE teaching + collect/report data; UAP may REPORT observations — neither assesses/interprets/decides. Charge role: keep judgment + assessment + evaluation + initial teaching as RN work.

labs.v: The newly admitted patient with active suicidal ideation and a PLAN is on 1:1 CONSTANT OBSERVATION — continuous, arm's-length, line-of-sight supervision that is never interrupted, including in the bathroom, with environmental safety measures (remove ligature points, cords, sharps, belts/shoelaces, glass, and other means; search belongings per policy). The RN performs the SUICIDE-RISK ASSESSMENT and develops the SAFETY PLAN (these are RN-only). The continuous OBSERVATION itself CAN be delegated to a trained UAP/psychiatric technician — but only with the RIGHT DIRECTION (explicit parameters: never leave the patient, maintain constant visual contact, what behaviors/statements to report immediately, no distractions/phones) and the RIGHT SUPERVISION (the RN checks in, ensures relief for breaks without leaving the patient unobserved, and remains ACCOUNTABLE for the patient's safety). The observer reports observations; the RN interprets them and re-assesses risk. Continuity of the 1:1 must be protected even during a unit emergency — coverage is reassigned, never abandoned.
labs.r: 1:1 constant observation = continuous arm's-length/line-of-sight, even in the bathroom + means-restriction (remove ligatures/cords/sharps/belts). RN does the suicide-risk ASSESSMENT + SAFETY PLAN (RN-only). The OBSERVATION may be delegated to a trained UAP with explicit parameters + RN supervision; RN stays ACCOUNTABLE. Observer REPORTS; RN interprets/re-assesses. Never break 1:1 coverage — reassign during emergencies. Charge role: protect 1:1 continuity.

labs.n: THE AGGRESSION/ESCALATION CYCLE + VERBAL DE-ESCALATION FIRST
labs.v: Behavioral emergencies follow a recognizable ESCALATION CYCLE — a TRIGGERING phase, an ESCALATION phase (rising agitation: pacing, loud/pressured speech, intrusiveness, clenched fists, refusing redirection — Mr. D's current state), a CRISIS/peak, and a recovery/post-crisis phase. The goal is to intervene EARLY, during escalation, with the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE intervention: VERBAL DE-ESCALATION. Key techniques: designate ONE calm spokesperson (avoid multiple voices/crowding); maintain a calm, low tone and non-threatening posture; RESPECT personal space (stay outside arm's reach, at an angle, hands visible); LISTEN and acknowledge the patient's feelings/concerns; LOWER environmental stimulation (reduce noise, move other patients away, dim the area); set CLEAR, simple LIMITS without arguing or power-struggling; and offer realistic CHOICES, including an ORAL PRN medication and a quieter space. De-escalation preserves dignity and the therapeutic relationship and frequently prevents the need for any chemical or physical restraint.
labs.r: Escalation cycle: triggering → ESCALATION (pacing, loud/pressured speech, intrusive, clenched fists) → crisis → recovery. Intervene EARLY with VERBAL DE-ESCALATION (least restrictive): ONE calm spokesperson, low tone, RESPECT personal space (outside arm's reach, hands visible), LISTEN/validate, LOWER stimulation, set clear limits without arguing, offer CHOICES (oral PRN, quiet space). Preserves dignity + often prevents restraint. Charge role: lead verbal de-escalation first.

labs.n: STAFF + ENVIRONMENTAL SAFETY during a behavioral emergency
labs.v: While de-escalating, the charge nurse simultaneously safeguards staff, the patient, and other patients. SUMMON HELP early — activate the unit's behavioral-response procedure so adequate trained staff are present (a show of organized, calm support; never confront an escalating patient ALONE). Position for SAFETY: keep your OWN clear path to an exit, do NOT corner or block the patient's egress (a cornered patient is more likely to strike out), stand at an angle outside arm's reach, and avoid turning your back. Reduce the ENVIRONMENT's danger: quietly move OTHER patients away from the area (their safety and dignity, and removal of an audience that can fuel escalation), and remove or secure objects that could be used as weapons. Assign clear roles (who speaks, who moves patients, who retrieves medication, who calls the provider) using closed-loop communication. Continuous 1:1 and other high-acuity coverage must be maintained throughout — reassign, do not abandon.
labs.r: During escalation: SUMMON HELP/behavioral response early — never confront alone. Keep YOUR exit path; do NOT corner the patient (cornering provokes striking out); stand at an angle, outside arm's reach, don't turn your back. Move OTHER patients away + remove possible weapons. Assign clear roles (speaker, mover, med, provider call) with closed-loop communication. Maintain 1:1/high-acuity coverage — reassign, never abandon. Charge role: staff + environmental safety.

labs.n: PRN / EMERGENCY MEDICATION — least-restrictive route first
labs.v: Medication during agitation follows the LEAST-RESTRICTIVE principle. OFFER an ORAL (PO) PRN medication FIRST when the patient can still participate — oral administration is LESS restrictive than intramuscular and preserves autonomy (e.g., oral olanzapine or another ordered agent; oral concentrate/ODT options aid acceptance). IM EMERGENCY MEDICATION is reserved for when oral is REFUSED AND the patient poses an IMMINENT danger to self or others that verbal de-escalation has not controlled; common regimens include an antipsychotic (e.g., haloperidol) ± a benzodiazepine (e.g., lorazepam) ± an anticholinergic (e.g., diphenhydramine or benztropine to prevent dystonia) — NOTE: avoid combining IM olanzapine with IM benzodiazepine (excess sedation/cardiorespiratory depression). After ANY sedating medication, MONITOR sedation level, airway/respirations, vital signs, and for extrapyramidal symptoms (acute dystonia/akathisia), and DOCUMENT the indication, attempts at less-restrictive measures, the drug/dose/route, and the response. The RN (not the UAP) decides on, administers/oversees, and evaluates emergency medication.
labs.r: Least-restrictive ROUTE: ORAL PRN FIRST while the patient can participate (oral < IM). IM emergency meds ONLY when oral refused AND imminent danger persists despite de-escalation — antipsychotic (haloperidol) ± benzo (lorazepam) ± anticholinergic (diphenhydramine/benztropine); AVOID IM olanzapine + IM benzo (sedation/cardioresp risk). After sedation: monitor LOC/airway/VS/EPS + document indication, lesser measures tried, drug/dose/route, response. RN decides/evaluates, not UAP. Charge role: oral before IM.

labs.n: SECLUSION / RESTRAINT — the absolute LAST resort
labs.v: Seclusion and physical restraint are the MOST restrictive interventions and are used ONLY as a LAST resort, when the patient poses an imminent danger to self or others and ALL less-restrictive measures (verbal de-escalation, environmental changes, offered oral medication) have failed or are clearly inadequate. Requirements (CMS/Joint Commission): a PROVIDER ORDER that is NOT PRN/standing and is TIME-LIMITED (adult restraint orders generally up to 4 hours, with renewal and required in-person evaluation within set timeframes); the LEAST restrictive effective method; CONTINUOUS or very frequent monitoring of safety, circulation, respiration, hydration, toileting, and behavior; ongoing assessment of readiness for the EARLIEST possible discontinuation (release criteria, not the clock); and a POST-EVENT DEBRIEF with the patient and staff. Restraint is NOT a behavior-management or staffing convenience and is never the first response to pacing or a raised voice. (See the dedicated restraint-safety case for the full standard.) On a psychiatric unit, the emphasis is preventing the need for restraint through early de-escalation.
labs.r: Seclusion/restraint = MOST restrictive = LAST resort: only for imminent danger AFTER less-restrictive measures fail. Needs a TIME-LIMITED, NON-PRN provider order; least-restrictive method; CONTINUOUS monitoring (safety/circulation/respiration/hydration/toileting); release at the EARLIEST safe point (criteria, not clock); post-event DEBRIEF. NOT for convenience and never the first response to pacing/raised voice. Charge role: exhaust least-restrictive first; restraint last.

labs.v: Throughout, the charge nurse leads with CLOSED-LOOP communication (give a clear directive, have it read back, confirm completion), supervises and EVALUATES delegated work (e.g., verifies the technician maintained the 1:1 with the correct parameters; confirms the LPN/LVN gave scheduled medications and reported the CIWA findings for the RN to interpret), and stays available to respond and re-prioritize. After the event, the charge nurse REASSESSES assignments and 1:1 coverage for the changed acuity, leads a POST-INCIDENT DEBRIEF with staff (what triggered it, what worked, lessons, emotional support/countertransference) and, when ready, with the patient (collaborative review and prevention planning), and ensures accurate, objective DOCUMENTATION of the escalation, the least-restrictive measures used, medications, monitoring, and outcomes. PROTECTIVE PRACTICES demonstrated: scope-appropriate assignments; delegation by the five rights with retained accountability; protecting the highest-acuity patients personally; early least-restrictive de-escalation (oral before IM, restraint avoided); staff/environmental safety; uninterrupted 1:1 coverage; and supervision, evaluation, debrief, and documentation. EXPECTED OUTCOME: the manic patient regains control without restraint, no one is injured, the suicidal patient remains safely observed, delegated tasks are completed correctly, and the team debriefs and adjusts.
labs.r: Charge leadership: CLOSED-LOOP communication, SUPERVISE + EVALUATE delegated work (verify 1:1 parameters, confirm LPN meds + reported data for RN to interpret), stay available to re-prioritize. Post-event: REASSESS assignments/1:1, lead a DEBRIEF (staff + patient) + objective DOCUMENTATION. PROTECTIVE: scope-appropriate assignments, five-rights delegation with retained accountability, RN keeps high-acuity, early least-restrictive de-escalation, staff/milieu safety, unbroken 1:1, supervision/evaluation/debrief. Charge role: lead, supervise, debrief, document.

nursesNote: Day-shift CHARGE RN, 20-bed adult inpatient PSYCHIATRIC unit, census 18. Team: charge RN + 1 staff RN + 1 LPN/LVN + 2 psychiatric techs (UAP). Board — Bed 4: new overnight admit on 1:1 CONSTANT OBSERVATION for active SI with a plan; Bed 7: 'Mr. D,' 34, bipolar I ACUTE MANIA, now ESCALATING (pacing, loud/pressured speech, intrusive, demanding the phone, fists clenching, won't sit for breakfast); Bed 9: first-episode psychosis, guarded, responding to internal stimuli; Bed 12: borderline PD, splitting/limit-testing; Bed 15: alcohol withdrawal on CIWA-Ar; plus several stable patients pending discharge. TWO competencies: SAFE DELEGATION/ASSIGNMENT + BEHAVIORAL DE-ESCALATION LEADERSHIP. SCOPE — RN ONLY (never delegated): assessment, analysis/judgment, planning, EVALUATION of response, INITIAL teaching, UNSTABLE/complex patients (new SI risk assessment + safety plan, escalating mania, first-episode-psychosis assessment, CIWA benzo titration, emergency-med decision). LPN/LVN (under RN): routine PO/IM/SQ meds, REINFORCE prior teaching, data collection/REPORTING, STABLE/PREDICTABLE patients. UAP/tech: 1:1 OBSERVATION (clear parameters + RN oversight), vitals, ADLs, transport, milieu — NO assessment/teaching/evaluation/judgment/titration. FIVE RIGHTS of delegation: right TASK, CIRCUMSTANCE, PERSON, DIRECTION/communication (closed-loop), SUPERVISION/EVALUATION — RN RETAINS ACCOUNTABILITY after delegating; 'busy' never justifies unsafe delegation. ASSIGNMENT = match ACUITY to COMPETENCY; RN keeps high-acuity/unpredictable, stay available to supervise + respond, REASSESS as acuity changes. 1:1 SI patient: continuous arm's-length/line-of-sight (even bathroom) + means-restriction (remove ligatures/cords/sharps/belts); RN does the risk assessment + safety plan; observation may be delegated to a trained tech with explicit parameters + RN supervision; never break coverage. DE-ESCALATION (Mr. D): recognize escalation EARLY → VERBAL DE-ESCALATION FIRST (one calm spokesperson, low tone, RESPECT personal space/outside arm's reach/hands visible, LISTEN/validate, LOWER stimulation, clear limits without arguing, offer CHOICES incl. ORAL PRN + quiet space). SAFETY: summon help/behavioral response early (never confront alone), keep YOUR exit, do NOT corner the patient, move other patients away + remove possible weapons, assign roles with closed-loop comm, maintain 1:1/high-acuity coverage. MEDICATION least-restrictive ROUTE: ORAL PRN FIRST (oral < IM); IM emergency meds (haloperidol ± lorazepam ± diphenhydramine; avoid IM olanzapine + IM benzo) ONLY if oral refused AND imminent danger persists → then monitor LOC/airway/VS/EPS + document. SECLUSION/RESTRAINT = LAST resort: imminent danger after less-restrictive fails, time-limited NON-PRN provider order, continuous monitoring, earliest safe release, post-event debrief — never for convenience or a raised voice. CHARGE leadership: closed-loop communication, SUPERVISE + EVALUATE delegated work, reassess assignments/1:1 after the event, lead DEBRIEF (staff + patient) + objective DOCUMENTATION. OUTCOME: Mr. D accepted oral olanzapine + quiet space, de-escalated WITHOUT restraint; no injuries; 1:1 maintained; delegated tasks completed; team debriefed and assignments adjusted.
providerOrders: SETTING: 20-bed adult inpatient psychiatric unit; charge RN leads RN + LPN/LVN + 2 psychiatric techs (UAP); make safe scope-appropriate assignments and supervise the team

1:1 CONSTANT OBSERVATION (Bed 4): continuous arm's-length/line-of-sight suicide precautions including bathroom; environmental means-restriction (remove ligatures/cords/sharps/belts/glass); RN performs suicide-risk assessment and safety plan; observation may be delegated to a trained technician with explicit parameters and RN supervision; never interrupt coverage

DELEGATION: follow the five rights (right task, circumstance, person, direction/communication, supervision/evaluation); RN retains accountability; do NOT delegate assessment, analysis, planning, evaluation, initial teaching, unstable-patient care, or judgment (e.g., CIWA-based benzodiazepine titration, emergency-medication decisions)

BEHAVIORAL ESCALATION (Bed 7, acute mania): recognize escalation early; lead VERBAL DE-ESCALATION first (single calm spokesperson, respect personal space, lower stimulation, listen, set clear limits, offer choices including an oral PRN); least-restrictive continuum

SAFETY: activate the behavioral-response procedure; never confront alone; maintain staff egress and do not corner the patient; move other patients away and remove possible weapons; assign roles with closed-loop communication

PRN/EMERGENCY MEDICATION: offer ORAL PRN first (e.g., olanzapine) while the patient can participate; reserve IM emergency medication (antipsychotic ± benzodiazepine ± anticholinergic; avoid IM olanzapine + IM benzodiazepine) for refused oral with imminent danger; monitor sedation/airway/VS/EPS and document

SECLUSION/RESTRAINT: last resort only for imminent danger after less-restrictive measures fail; requires a time-limited, non-PRN provider order, continuous monitoring, earliest safe discontinuation, and a post-event debrief

ALCOHOL WITHDRAWAL (Bed 15): CIWA-Ar monitoring; the technician may obtain vitals and report findings, the LPN/LVN may collect data and administer ordered medications within scope, but the RN interprets CIWA scores and directs symptom-triggered benzodiazepine dosing

SUPERVISION/EVALUATION: charge RN supervises and evaluates all delegated tasks (verify 1:1 parameters maintained; confirm scheduled medications given and data reported for RN interpretation); stay available to respond and re-prioritize

POST-EVENT: reassess assignments and 1:1 coverage for changed acuity; lead a post-incident debrief with staff and (when ready) the patient; ensure accurate, objective documentation of the escalation, least-restrictive measures, medications, monitoring, and outcomes

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What matters for safe assignments and the escalating patient?
steps.inst: Select ALL findings/considerations that are clinically significant.

opts.text: Mr. D (acute mania) is escalating — pacing, loud pressured speech, intrusiveness, clenched fists, rising HR/BP — the escalation phase calling for early verbal de-escalation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — recognizing escalation early allows the least-restrictive intervention before crisis. Charge role: lead de-escalation now.

opts.text: The newly admitted patient with active suicidal ideation and a plan requires continuous 1:1 observation and a safe environment, with RN risk assessment and safety planning
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the highest-acuity safety need; the RN performs the assessment/plan while observation may be delegated with parameters. Charge role: protect 1:1 coverage.

opts.text: The staff mix (charge RN, staff RN, LPN/LVN, 2 psychiatric techs) and the varied patient acuity must be matched in scope-appropriate assignments
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — assignment-making matches acuity to competency. Charge role: safe assignments.

opts.text: Delegation must follow scope of practice and the five rights, and the RN remains accountable for delegated tasks
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the framework for every safe delegation. Charge role: delegate by the five rights, retain accountability.

opts.text: Verbal de-escalation and least-restrictive measures (including an offered oral PRN) come before any IM medication or restraint
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — the least-restrictive continuum guides the behavioral response. Charge role: verbal/oral before IM/restraint.

opts.text: Staff and environmental safety — summoning help, keeping exits, removing other patients and dangerous objects — is essential during escalation
opts.rat: PRIORITY CUE — protecting staff, the patient, and peers is concurrent with de-escalation. Charge role: ensure safety.

opts.text: Immediately place Mr. D in physical restraints and give an IM injection as the first response to his pacing and loud voice
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraint is a last resort; pacing and a raised voice call for early verbal de-escalation and an offered oral PRN, not a first-line restraint.

opts.text: Delegate the initial suicide-risk assessment of the new patient and the assessment of Mr. D's escalation to a psychiatric technician
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Assessment and clinical judgment cannot be delegated to a UAP; the RN performs risk assessment and evaluates the escalating patient.

opts.text: Have the unlicensed technician independently interpret CIWA-Ar scores and titrate the alcohol-withdrawal patient's benzodiazepine dose
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Interpreting CIWA and titrating medication require nursing judgment and are outside UAP scope; the RN directs dosing (the tech may obtain vitals and report).

steps.title: Analyze Cues
steps.sub: What delegation, scope, and de-escalation principles apply?

opts.text: The RN cannot delegate assessment, analysis, planning, evaluation, initial teaching, or care of unstable patients — these require nursing judgment
opts.rat: CORRECT — defines RN-only responsibilities. Foundation for safe assignment.

opts.text: The five rights of delegation (right task, circumstance, person, direction/communication, supervision/evaluation) guide delegation, and the RN keeps accountability
opts.rat: CORRECT — the delegation framework with retained accountability. Foundation for safe delegation.

opts.text: A trained UAP/psychiatric technician can perform constant 1:1 observation, obtain vital signs, assist with ADLs, and transport — with clear parameters and RN oversight
opts.rat: CORRECT — UAP scope with direction and supervision. Foundation for appropriate task assignment.

opts.text: An LPN/LVN can administer most routine PO/IM/SQ medications, reinforce previously taught content, and collect/report data for stable patients under RN supervision
opts.rat: CORRECT — LPN/LVN scope. Foundation for matching stable, predictable work.

opts.text: Verbal de-escalation (one calm spokesperson, respect personal space, lower stimulation, set limits, offer choices) is first-line; restraint/seclusion is last resort
opts.rat: CORRECT — least-restrictive de-escalation continuum. Foundation for the behavioral response.

opts.text: Offering an oral medication is less restrictive than IM; seclusion/restraint requires a time-limited, non-PRN provider order and continuous monitoring
opts.rat: CORRECT — least-restrictive route and the restraint standard. Foundation for safe, lawful intervention.

opts.text: Because the unit is busy, the charge nurse may delegate patient assessments and clinical judgment to the UAP to save time
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Workload never authorizes delegating assessment or judgment; these remain RN responsibilities regardless of how busy the unit is.

opts.text: Physical restraint is the appropriate first intervention whenever a psychiatric patient raises their voice or paces the hall
opts.rat: ⚠️ WRONG. Restraint is a last resort for imminent danger after less-restrictive measures fail; early agitation calls for verbal de-escalation.

opts.text: Ensure immediate safety — lead de-escalation of the escalating manic patient and protect him, other patients, and staff
opts.rat: #1 — An escalating patient is an imminent danger to self and others; de-escalation and safety come first. Charge role: immediate safety.

opts.text: Maintain uninterrupted, safe 1:1 observation of the actively suicidal patient throughout the event
opts.rat: #2 — The highest standing safety need; coverage must never lapse, even during the escalation. Charge role: protect 1:1.

opts.text: Make and adjust assignments that match each team member's scope and competency to patient acuity
opts.rat: #3 — Safe assignments structure the whole shift and free the RN to respond. Charge role: acuity-to-competency matching.

opts.text: Delegate appropriate tasks to the LPN/LVN and technicians with clear direction and ongoing supervision
opts.rat: #4 — Appropriate delegation extends safe care, but follows the higher-priority safety and assignment decisions. Charge role: delegate + supervise.

opts.text: Document the event and lead a post-incident debrief with staff and the patient
opts.rat: #5 — Essential follow-through, performed after the immediate situation is safe. Charge role: debrief + document.

steps.title: Generate Solutions
steps.inst: For EACH task, classify the MOST appropriate team member: RN Only, LPN/LVN, or UAP/Tech.

opts.text: Perform the suicide-risk assessment of the newly admitted patient and develop the safety plan
opts.rat: Assessment and care planning require nursing judgment — RN-only. Charge role: keep risk assessment as RN work.

opts.text: Lead verbal de-escalation of the escalating manic patient and decide on PRN/emergency medication
opts.rat: Real-time judgment, assessment of danger, and medication decisions are RN responsibilities. Charge role: lead de-escalation.

opts.text: Evaluate the patient's response to a PRN medication and revise the plan of care
opts.rat: Evaluation of response and revising the plan require nursing judgment — RN-only. Charge role: evaluate outcomes.

opts.text: Provide initial teaching to a newly admitted patient about the unit, the safety plan, and new medications
opts.rat: Initial teaching of new content is RN-only; an LPN/LVN may later reinforce it. Charge role: initial teaching.

opts.text: Administer the scheduled oral mood stabilizer and a routine ordered IM antipsychotic to a stable patient
opts.rat: Routine medication administration to a stable patient is within LPN/LVN scope under RN supervision.

opts.text: Reinforce previously taught coping skills and collect/report self-reported symptom data for the RN to interpret
opts.rat: Reinforcing prior teaching and collecting/reporting data are within LPN/LVN scope; the RN interprets the data.

opts.text: Perform the CIWA-Ar symptom checklist on the alcohol-withdrawal patient and report the findings to the RN
opts.rat: Structured data collection and reporting are within LPN/LVN scope; the RN interprets the score and directs dosing.

opts.text: Provide continuous 1:1 line-of-sight observation of the suicidal patient and report any concerning statements or behavior
opts.rat: Constant observation with clear parameters and reporting is within UAP scope, with RN oversight and retained accountability.

opts.text: Obtain and record routine vital signs and assist patients with ADLs and hygiene
opts.rat: Vital-sign measurement and ADL assistance are routine tasks within UAP scope.

opts.text: Accompany a stable patient to the activity room and help maintain a calm, low-stimulation milieu
opts.rat: Escort, supervision of stable patients, and milieu support are within UAP scope.

steps.title: Take Action
steps.sub: Sequence the response to the escalation
steps.inst: Rank the order of the charge nurse's actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Summon help/activate the behavioral response and ensure safety — keep exits clear, do not corner the patient, and move other patients and dangerous objects away
opts.rat: #1 — FIRST. Adequate staff and a safe environment must be in place before anything else; never confront alone. Charge role: safety setup.

opts.text: Lead verbal de-escalation — one calm spokesperson, respect personal space, lower stimulation, listen, and set clear limits
opts.rat: #2 — The least-restrictive intervention; frequently resolves the escalation. Charge role: verbal de-escalation.

opts.text: Offer an oral PRN medication and a quiet, low-stimulation space to help him regain control
opts.rat: #3 — Oral medication is less restrictive than IM and is offered while the patient can still participate. Charge role: oral PRN + quiet space.

opts.text: If he refuses oral medication and danger is imminent, administer the ordered IM emergency medication with monitoring
opts.rat: #4 — Escalate to IM only when oral is refused and danger persists; then monitor sedation/airway/VS/EPS. Charge role: IM if needed.

opts.text: Reserve seclusion/restraint for last resort with a time-limited order, then reassess assignments, debrief, and document
opts.rat: #5 — Restraint is the final option for imminent danger after all else fails; follow with reassessment, debrief, and documentation. Charge role: last resort + follow-through.

steps.title: Evaluate Outcomes
steps.inst: For each action/outcome, classify the charge nurse's practice.

opts.text: The charge nurse kept the new patient's suicide-risk assessment, safety planning, and the de-escalation/medication decisions as RN responsibilities
opts.rat: Assessment, planning, evaluation, and judgment are RN-only and were correctly retained. Appropriate practice.

opts.text: Verbal de-escalation plus an offered oral PRN and a quiet space helped Mr. D regain control without IM medication or restraint
opts.rat: The least-restrictive continuum succeeded — the ideal outcome. Appropriate practice.

opts.text: The technician was assigned continuous 1:1 observation with explicit parameters and RN oversight, and reported behaviors for the RN to interpret
opts.rat: Observation is delegable to a trained UAP with direction and supervision; the RN retains accountability and interprets. Appropriate practice.

opts.text: The LPN/LVN administered the scheduled oral mood stabilizer, reinforced coping skills, and reported CIWA findings within scope
opts.rat: Routine medications, reinforcing teaching, and data reporting are within LPN/LVN scope under RN supervision. Appropriate practice.

opts.text: After the event, the charge nurse reassessed assignments and 1:1 coverage and led a debrief with staff and the patient
opts.rat: Reassessment, supervision, and debrief are core charge-nurse follow-through. Appropriate practice.

opts.text: The charge nurse delegated the newly admitted suicidal patient's risk assessment to a psychiatric technician
opts.rat: ⚠️ Assessment and clinical judgment cannot be delegated to a UAP; this is unsafe and outside scope. Inappropriate practice.

opts.text: Mr. D was placed in restraints and given an IM injection immediately for pacing and a raised voice, skipping verbal de-escalation
opts.rat: ⚠️ Restraint is a last resort; early agitation warrants verbal de-escalation and an offered oral PRN first. Inappropriate practice.

opts.text: The technician was told to independently interpret CIWA scores and adjust the alcohol-withdrawal patient's benzodiazepine dose
opts.rat: ⚠️ Interpreting CIWA and titrating medication require nursing judgment and exceed UAP scope. Inappropriate practice.

opts.text: The charge nurse delegated tasks without giving clear direction, parameters, or any follow-up supervision
opts.rat: ⚠️ Violates the right direction/communication and supervision/evaluation; delegation without clear direction and oversight is unsafe. Inappropriate practice.

opts.text: Other patients were left in the dayroom near the escalating patient with no plan for their safety or removal
opts.rat: ⚠️ Failing to safeguard other patients during a behavioral emergency endangers them and can fuel escalation. Inappropriate practice.

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Management of Care, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
